<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Listing api endpoints**

I'm documenting the complete list of API endpoints from movies.ts including getTrend, getPopular, getTopRated, and getUpcoming. </details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /Users/rishabhgupta/ReactCursor/OttStreamingApp/Task2/StreamList/src/hooks/useMovieDetail.ts</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

In this repo, **all TMDB HTTP calls are defined in one place**, not spread across a separate “API doc” file.

## Where they live

| Location | Role |
|----------|------|
| **`Task2/StreamList/src/api/movies.ts`** | Every **endpoint + function** (`getTrending`, `searchMovies`, etc.). This is the full list of APIs the app uses. |
| **`Task2/StreamList/src/api/client.ts`** | Shared **Axios instance**: `TMDB_BASE_URL`, Bearer token, retries — no per-route URLs here. |
| **`Task2/StreamList/src/api/types.ts`** | **Response TypeScript types** (`Movie`, `MovieDetail`, …), not URLs. |
| **`Task2/StreamList/README.md`** | Describes the **pattern** (“one client”, calls through `movies.ts`) and env vars; it does **not** enumerate every path. |

The README explicitly says TMDB requests go through **`src/api/movies.ts`** (and the tree shows `api/` for TMDB calls).

---

## All APIs used by the project (from `movies.ts`)

These are the **only** `apiClient.get(...)` routes in the app:

1. **`getTrending`** → `GET /trending/movie/week` (params: `language`, `page`)
2. **`getTopRated`** → `GET /movie/top_rated` (params: `language`, `page`)
3. **`getGenres`** → `GET /genre/movie/list` (params: `language`)
4. **`getDiscoverByGenre`** → `GET /discover/movie` (params: `language`, `page`, `with_genres`)
5. **`searchMovies`** → `GET /search/movie` (params: `query`, `page`; optional `AbortSignal`)
6. **`getMovieDetail`** → `GET /movie/{id}` (params: `language`)
7. **`getMovieCredits`** → `GET /movie/{id}/credits` (params: `language`)
8. **`getSimilarMovies`** → `GET /movie/{id}/similar` (params: `language`, `page: 1`)

There is **no second API module** under `src/api/` in this project; anything that talks to TMDB should go through these (per your project rules, via `client.ts` only).

---

## Who calls what (quick map)

- **`useHome.ts`** → `getGenres`, `getTopRated`, `getTrending`
- **`useSearch.ts`** → `searchMovies`
- **`useMovieDetail.ts`** → `getMovieDetail`, `getMovieCredits`, `getSimilarMovies`
- **`SearchScreen.tsx`** → `getTrending` (empty-state / default content per your earlier grep)
- **`HomeGenreDiscoverSection.tsx`** → `getDiscoverByGenre`
- **`BecauseSavedRow.tsx`** → `getSimilarMovies`

So: **the single source of truth for “all APIs” is `src/api/movies.ts`**; the README only documents the **architecture**, not a full route list.

---

_**Agent (default)**_

In **this StreamList codebase there is no `console.warn` (and no `console.log`)** in your app source.

The only explicit `console` usage under `Task2/StreamList` is:

```50:50:Task2/StreamList/src/components/common/ScreenErrorBoundary.tsx
    console.error('ScreenErrorBoundary', error, errorInfo.componentStack);
```

That runs **only when the error boundary catches a render error**, not on a normal cold start.

So the **“Debug” warning you see when opening the app is almost certainly not from a `console.warn` you (or this repo) added**. It usually comes from:

1. **React Native’s LogBox** showing a **framework or library** warning (React Navigation, Reanimated, Screens, Safe Area, deprecated props, etc.).
2. **Metro** or other tooling (less often shown as the in-app yellow banner).

To see the real source, open the warning in the LogBox UI and read the **full message and stack**; that will name the package or API (e.g. “[deprecation] …”).

**Summary:** Nothing in your project is calling `console.warn` on startup; only `console.error` exists, and only inside `ScreenErrorBoundary` on errors. If you paste the **exact warning text** from the simulator/device, we can point to the dependency or line that triggers it.

_**Agent (default)**_

That banner is **not something you added in the StreamList app**. There is **no** string like `"Open debugger to view warnings"` anywhere under your `src/` folder, and it is not coming from your `console.warn` / `console.log` (you barely use `console` at all).

## What it actually is

It is **React Native’s dev-only LogBox** (the same system that shows yellow “Warning” toasts). In some setups it shows that **generic line** when it wants you to read the **full warning text** in:

- the **Metro** terminal where you ran `npm start` / `yarn start`, or  
- the **Chrome / Flipper / React Native DevTools** JS debugger if you have that attached.

So the **text of the toast is defined inside the `react-native` package** (LogBox / dev overlay), not in your project files.

## What you should do to see the real cause

1. **Tap the yellow toast** — it often opens the full LogBox screen with the real message and stack.  
2. **Watch the Metro terminal** — the same warnings are usually printed there with the full text.  
3. **Open React Native DevTools** (or attach the debugger) — as the banner says, the **details** of *which* warning fired live there.

The **underlying** warning is usually from **React Navigation**, **Screens**, **Safe Area**, **Reanimated**, **deprecated props**, **keys on lists**, etc.—not from a line in your repo that says “Open debugger…”.

**Bottom line:** You will not find `"Open debugger to view warnings"` in your app code; it is the framework’s dev UI telling you to look at the debugger/terminal for the **actual** warning that triggered it. If you paste the **full warning** from Metro or from tapping the toast, we can tie it to a specific library or screen.

---

_**User**_

if i want to remove then how i can 

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Explaining logbox removal**

I'm explaining how to remove the LogBox toast in React Native without implementing any solutions. </details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

You have two different goals people mix up here:

## 1. Make the banner go away properly (recommended)

The toast appears because **something is calling `console.warn` or triggering a framework warning**. Fixing that removes the banner without hiding real problems.

- Tap the toast or check the **Metro terminal** for the **full warning text**.
- Fix that issue (wrong prop, missing key, navigation config, deprecated API, etc.).

That is the right long-term approach.

---

## 2. Hide LogBox / warnings in development (possible, not ideal)

React Native exposes **`LogBox`** (import from `'react-native'`). Common patterns:

**Ignore specific messages** (narrowest):

```ts
import { LogBox } from 'react-native';

LogBox.ignoreLogs([
  'Exact substring or regex pattern from the warning',
]);
```

Usually done once at startup (e.g. in `index.js` or top of `App.tsx`), often wrapped in `if (__DEV__) { ... }`.

**Ignore all logs** (very blunt — hides errors too in some setups; easy to miss real bugs):

```ts
import { LogBox } from 'react-native';

LogBox.ignoreAllLogs(true);
```

There is also **`LogBox.uninstall()`** in some RN versions to tear down the overlay entirely; behavior can vary by RN version, so check the [LogBox docs](https://reactnative.dev/docs/debugging#logbox) for your exact `react-native` version.

**Tradeoffs:** Hiding LogBox does **not** remove the underlying problem; it only stops the UI from nagging you. Teams usually use `ignoreLogs` only for **known, safe, third-party noise** they cannot fix yet.

---

## 3. Release builds

In a **production release** (not `__DEV__`), users typically **do not** see this dev LogBox UI. If you only care about end users, ship a release build; the dev overlay is for development.

---

**Summary:** To “remove” it in a healthy way, **resolve the warning** Metro/debugger shows. To suppress it in dev only, use **`LogBox.ignoreLogs`** (targeted) or **`LogBox.ignoreAllLogs`** (heavy). I’m in **Ask mode** so I can’t edit your repo; if you want this wired into `index.js` / `App.tsx` for you, switch to **Agent mode** and say whether you want “ignore one known message” vs “disable LogBox entirely.”
